competition.narrative: SAP Ariba is the primary competitor as the incumbent, but AGL is actively evaluating alternatives to replace it ('need a way to penetrate Ariba', Next Step 21-May). The 19-Jun Next Step states Sasith 'needs to demonstrate he has considered alterant options', confirming a formal alternatives evaluation is in progress (June/July 2026), but we have zero visibility into what is being compared, at what price, or against what scorecard. Likely alternatives: (1) Coupa (a known Ariba competitor, and Sasith used Coupa at a prior employer per earlier call context), (2) lighter spend-only tools (Sievo, Zip, Ivalua) that may come in significantly cheaper than Zycus's $90k platform investment, (3) do-nothing / keep Ariba + custom spend cube and avoid the cost. At $90k ACV for a full platform (sourcing + spend + AI: iSave, iAnalyze, Merlin), Zycus is priced as a premium solution. The wedge is platform consolidation (one system, one integration, sourcing + spend + AI in one, not two point solutions), AI depth (Anna autonomous negotiation agent, AutoClass classification, Merlin intelligence vs basic reporting), and ROI (time saved per sourcing event, analyst hours freed, benefits-tracking automation). However, without knowing what alternatives are being evaluated and at what price, we are positioning blind. The 50% confidence in the Next Step likely reflects pricing/approval uncertainty: if a lighter tool or do-nothing comes in cheaper and we have not quantified our ROI, cost becomes the blocker. How we win: deliver a quantified ROI model that frames the $90k investment as self-funding within X months (time saved, headcount avoided, benefits captured), emphasize platform value vs multi-tool cost and complexity, and position AI depth (Anna, Merlin) as the differentiator for an AI-hungry buyer. We must also discover what alternatives are on the table and position our wedge directly against them.

identify_pain.narrative: The operational and financial pain is confirmed and articulated by Sasith (Data Manager). AGL's procurement and sourcing teams are frustrated with SAP Ariba: too expensive, poor usability, and the sourcing team was excluded from a recent Ariba uplift, leaving them without the tools they need. Sasith is the pain owner for spend visibility and data management (his role is Data Manager, Procurement & Property), and he is championing the deal to replace Ariba with a platform that delivers better spend analysis, sourcing efficiency, and AI-driven automation (Anna negotiation agent, AutoClass classification, Merlin intelligence). The 21-May Next Step notes 'good rapport with business leaders- need a way to penetrate Ariba- champion is coaching', confirming Ariba-replacement positioning is central. The sourcing team (primary pain owners for event management and benefits tracking) has not been engaged directly, but their pain is inferred from the Ariba-exclusion context and the product scope (iSave sourcing module). The compelling event is weak: no contract expiration, no regulatory deadline, no burning platform. The driver is operational frustration (manual work, poor Ariba UX, lack of AI automation) and the GM's mid-July funding cycle, but this is an improvement initiative, not a must-fix crisis. The 50% confidence in the Next Step suggests the pain is real but the urgency and funding commitment are uncertain.

items.detail: At $90k ACV this is a platform investment (sourcing + spend + AI modules), and AGL is conducting an alternatives evaluation ('needs to demonstrate he has considered alterant options', Next Step 19 Jun) with no visibility into what is being compared or at what price. Risk: Zycus is priced as a premium platform; if a lighter spend-only tool (Sievo, Zip) or do-nothing (keep Ariba + custom cube) comes in significantly cheaper and the ROI is not quantified, cost becomes the blocker. The 50% confidence in the Next Step likely reflects pricing/approval uncertainty. Mitigation: deliver a quantified ROI (time saved, analyst hours freed, benefits captured) and emphasize platform value (one system, one integration, sourcing + spend + AI, not two point solutions).

No engagement with the Economic Buyer (GM, head of procurement) or the sourcing team (primary pain owners). The deal is single-threaded through Sasith (Data Manager, Evaluator), who has submitted the business case but does not hold approval authority. The GM and head of procurement will decide mid-July+ whether to fund this, and we have no direct read on their priorities, budget, or alternatives preference. Risk: if the GM or head of procurement is cost-focused, prefers do-nothing, or has a different priority, the deal stalls or loses and we learn too late. Mitigation: request head-of-procurement intro on the early-July callback to validate business case and pricing before the mid-July funding window.

The 30 September 2026 close date is not credible. Buyer timeline: funding decisions mid-July, Sasith returns 3 August, business case refinement if needed, alternatives evaluation (in progress June/July), head-of-procurement approval, contracting. That sequence realistically closes late Q4 2026 or Q1 2027, not 30 Sep 2026. The deal is healthy but the date will slip 60 to 90 days. Risk: if the forecast is held to 30 Sep and the deal does not close on time, it will be marked as slipped/at-risk even though it is progressing normally.

The sourcing team (primary users and pain owners for the Ariba-replacement use case) has not been engaged in the evaluation. Sasith (Data Manager) is championing the deal from the data/spend-analysis angle, but the sourcing team's buy-in is not confirmed. Risk: if the sourcing team is not consulted, they may resist the change, question the ROI, or lobby for a different solution (or no change), and the head of procurement may defer to them. The 13 Mar call noted sourcing was 'not included in the Ariba uplift' and is now frustrated; if they feel excluded again, adoption risk is high. The Ariba entrenchment ('need a way to penetrate Ariba', Next Step 21 May) means the sourcing team's buy-in is make-or-break. flags: Single-threaded through Sasith (Data Manager, Evaluator role). No engagement with the Economic Buyer (GM, head of procurement), the sourcing team (primary pain owners for the Ariba-replacement use case), or any executive sponsor. With a $90k decision, proposal submitted, and mid-July funding window approaching, the lack of EB/DM engagement is a yellow flag. At this deal size and complexity (platform buy, SAP integration, cross-functional impact on sourcing + spend teams) a single-threaded evaluator motion is fragile. Recommend: request a head-of-procurement intro on the early-July callback to validate the business case and pricing, and ask Sasith to intro the sourcing lead so we can tailor the ROI to their pain (time saved per event, admin reduction). Frame both as 'ensuring we've addressed every question for your GM and your sourcing stakeholders' rather than going around her.

Alternatives evaluation in flight with no visibility into who/what is being considered. Next Step (19 Jun) states Sasith 'needs to demonstrate he has considered alterant options', and the 50% confidence signals approval is not certain. We do not know what alternatives are in the mix (Coupa re-emerged? a spend-only tool like Sievo? do-nothing?), what the comparison criteria are, or when the evaluation will close. This is a live competitive risk: cost-conscious buyers often default to do-nothing or a cheaper point solution when a platform price feels high. Recommend: on the early-July call ask directly 'what alternatives are you reviewing, and how can we help you frame the comparison?' and offer a one-page ROI + reference customer (utilities/energy, Ariba exit/augmentation) to anchor the value case and neutralize cost objections before the mid-July funding decision.

No quantified ROI or savings target, leaving the business case vulnerable to do-nothing or a cheaper alternative. Pain is well-articulated (2-day sourcing process, manual spend cube, spreadsheet benefits tracking) but the value case is qualitative. At $90k ACV the GM and head of procurement will ask 'what do we get for this investment?' If the answer is only 'better usability and cleaner data' with no time/cost/savings number, do-nothing wins. Recommend: co-author a quantified ROI model with Sasith before the GM presentation (mid-July): sourcing team time saved (1.5 days per event × annual event volume = X hours freed for strategic work), analyst team manual-handling reduction (Y hours/week freed from cube cleanup = Z annual hours to strategic analytics), and benefits-tracking leakage prevented (benefits currently in a spreadsheet, not captured in Ariba = estimated $ leakage per year). Deliver it as a one-page business-case support doc she can hand to the GM.

If the GM chooses do-nothing, it is because we did not make the ROI case, not because the solution is wrong.
